content:
# Luke Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 114-115 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_021_page_001.jpg, LUK_021_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having looked up, he saw the rich casting their gifts into the treasury.

**2** And he saw a certain poor widow casting in there two small copper coins,

**3** and he said, "Truly I say to you that this poor widow has cast in more than all of them;

**4** for all these out of their abundance cast in to the offerings, but she out of her poverty cast in all the livelihood that she had."

**5** And as some were speaking concerning the temple, that it was adorned with beautiful stones and offerings, he said,

**6** "These things which you behold — days will come in which there shall not be left stone upon stone which shall not be thrown down."

**7** And they questioned him, saying, "Teacher, when therefore shall these things be? And what is the sign when these things are about to come to pass?"

**8** And he said, "See that you be not led astray; for many shall come in my name, saying, 'I am he,' and, 'The time has drawn near.' Do not go after them.

**9** And when you hear of wars and tumults, be not terrified; for these things must come to pass first, but the end is not immediately."

**10** Then he said to them, "Nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom;

**11** and there shall be great earthquakes, and in various places famines and pestilences, and there shall be terrors and great signs from heaven.

**12** But before all these things they shall lay their hands upon you and shall persecute you, delivering you up to the synagogues and prisons, being brought before kings and governors on account of my name.

**13** It shall result for you as a testimony.

**14** Settle it therefore in your hearts not to prepare beforehand to make a defense;

**15** for I will give you a mouth and wisdom which all those who oppose you shall not be able to withstand or to contradict.

**16** And you shall be delivered up even by parents and brothers and kinsmen and friends, and they shall put to death some from among you,

**17** and you shall be hated by all on account of my name.

**18** And not a hair of your head shall by any means perish.

**19** In your endurance you shall gain your souls.

**20** But when you see Jerusalem being surrounded by armies, then know that her desolation has drawn near.

**21** Then let those in Judea flee to the mountains, and let those in the midst of her depart, and let not those in the regions enter into her;

**22** for these are days of vengeance, that all things which are written may be fulfilled.

**23** Woe to those who are with child and to those who nurse in those days! For there shall be great distress upon the land and wrath upon this people.

**24** And they shall fall by the edge of the sword, and shall be led captive into all the nations; and Jerusalem shall be trampled by the nations, until the times of the nations are fulfilled.

**25** And there shall be signs in sun and moon and stars, and upon the earth distress of nations in perplexity at the roaring of the sea and the waves,

**26** men fainting from fear and expectation of the things coming upon the world, for the powers of the heavens shall be shaken.

**27** And then they shall see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory.

**28** And when these things begin to come to pass, straighten yourselves and lift up your heads, because your redemption draws near."

**29** And he spoke a parable to them: "Behold the fig tree and all the trees;

**30** when they now put forth leaves, seeing it for yourselves, you know that the summer is already near.

**31** So also you, when you see these things coming to pass, know that the kingdom of God is near.

**32** Truly I say to you that this generation shall by no means pass away until all things come to pass.

**33** Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall by no means pass away.

**34** But take heed to yourselves, lest your hearts be weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness and the cares of this life, and that day come upon you suddenly,

**35** as a snare; for it shall come upon all those who dwell upon the face of all the earth.

**36** But be watchful at every time, making supplication that you may have strength to escape all these things that are about to come to pass, and to stand before the Son of Man."

**37** And he was teaching during the days in the temple, but during the nights he would go out and lodge on the mount called Olivet.

**38** And all the people would come early in the morning to him in the temple to hear him.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **21:1 — "the rich casting their gifts into the treasury" (tous ballontas eis to gazophylakion ta dōra autōn plousious):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥΣ ΒΑΛΛΟΝΤΑΣ ΕΙΣ ΤΟ ΓΑΖΟΦΥΛΑΚΙΟΝ ΤΑ ΔΩΡΑ ΑΥΤΩΝ ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΥΣ. The noun ΓΑΖΟΦΥΛΑΚΙΟΝ ("treasury") — from ΓΑΖΑ ("treasure," a Persian loanword) + ΦΥΛΑΚΗ ("guard, keeping") — designates the receptacle for offerings in the temple court, traditionally identified with the thirteen trumpet-shaped collection chests in the Court of the Women. The verb ΑΝΑΒΛΕΨΑΣ ("having looked up") — from ΑΝΑ ("up") + ΒΛΕΠΩ ("to see") — suggests that Jesus had been looking downward, perhaps seated and teaching, and raised his eyes to observe the scene. The chapter opens without a break from the denunciation of the scribes who "devour the houses of widows" (20:47) — the juxtaposition is deliberate: the widow of 21:2 is an exemplar of the very class the scribes exploit. The participial construction ΤΟΥΣ ΒΑΛΛΟΝΤΑΣ ("those casting") uses the present tense, depicting a continuous procession of donors.

2. **21:2 — "two small copper coins" (lepta duo):** Sinaiticus reads ΛΕΠΤΑ ΔΥΟ. The noun ΛΕΠΤΟΝ ("a lepton, a small coin") — from ΛΕΠΤΟΣ ("thin, fine, small") — was the smallest denomination in circulation, a tiny copper or bronze coin. Two lepta equaled one ΚΟΔΡΑΝΤΗΣ (Latin quadrans, one-sixty-fourth of a denarius). Mark 12:42 provides the Roman equivalence (ΔΥΟ ΛΕΠΤΑ Ο ΕΣΤΙΝ ΚΟΔΡΑΝΤΗΣ); Luke omits this gloss, perhaps because his audience already understood the coinage or because the emphasis falls not on the precise monetary value but on the widow's totality of giving. The adjective ΠΕΝΙΧΡΑ ("poor, needy") — from ΠΕΝΗΣ ("one who labors for a living, a poor person") — describes not destitution but the condition of one who must work to survive, making the sacrifice described in verse 4 all the more striking: she gives not from surplus but from subsistence.

3. **21:4 — "all the livelihood that she had" (panta ton bion hon eichen):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΟΝ ΒΙΟΝ ΟΝ ΕΙΧΕΝ. The noun ΒΙΟΣ ("livelihood, means of living") — distinct from ΖΩΗ ("life" in the biological or spiritual sense) — denotes the material resources by which one sustains life. The phrase ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΟΝ ΒΙΟΝ ("all the livelihood") is emphatic in its totality: not a portion, not a tithe, but everything she had to live on. The contrast established by the prepositions is precise: the rich gave ΕΚ ΤΟΥ ΠΕΡΙΣΣΕΥΟΝΤΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ ("out of their abundance," from ΠΕΡΙΣΣΕΥΩ, "to abound, to have more than enough"); the widow gave ΕΚ ΤΟΥ ΥΣΤΕΡΗΜΑΤΟΣ ΑΥΤΗΣ ("out of her poverty," from ΥΣΤΕΡΗΜΑ, "deficiency, lack, poverty"). The rich gave from overflow; the widow gave from emptiness. Jesus's judgment reverses conventional metrics of generosity: the amount matters less than the proportion, and the proportion matters less than the cost to the giver. Her two lepta constitute ΠΛΕΙΟΝ ΠΑΝΤΩΝ ("more than all") — not in quantity but in sacrifice.

4. **21:5 — "beautiful stones and offerings" (lithois kalois kai anathēmasin):** Sinaiticus reads ΛΙΘΟΙΣ ΚΑΛΟΙΣ ΚΑΙ ΑΝΑΘΗΜΑΣΙΝ. The noun ΑΝΑΘΗΜΑ ("offering, votive gift") — from ΑΝΑΤΙΘΗΜΙ ("to set up, to dedicate") — refers to the costly dedications adorning the temple: the golden vine over the entrance, the gifts of Herod, and other ornamental offerings. This word should be distinguished from ΑΝΑΘΕΜΑ ("a cursed thing"), though both derive from the same root. The adjective ΚΑΛΟΙΣ ("beautiful, noble, fine") applied to the stones refers to the massive, finely dressed blocks of the Herodian temple, some measuring over forty feet in length. Josephus describes the temple stones as white, enormous, and gleaming in the sun (Jewish War 5.222-224). The admiration expressed by "some" (ΤΙΝΩΝ) creates the dramatic contrast: they marvel at the temple's magnificence; Jesus announces its obliteration.

5. **21:6 — "there shall not be left stone upon stone" (ouk aphethēsetai lithos epi lithō):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΚ ΑΦΕΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ΛΙΘΟΣ ΕΠΙ ΛΙΘΩ. The verb ΑΦΙΗΜΙ ("to leave, to let go, to release") in the future passive ΑΦΕΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ means "shall be left" or "shall be allowed to remain." The double negative construction — ΟΥΚ ΑΦΕΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ... ΟΣ ΟΥ ΚΑΤΑΛΥΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall not be left ... which shall not be thrown down") — is emphatic: there will be absolutely no stone remaining upon another. The verb ΚΑΤΑΛΥΩ ("to throw down, to demolish, to destroy") — from ΚΑΤΑ ("down") + ΛΥΩ ("to loose") — describes the systematic dismantling of a structure. The prophecy was fulfilled in detail when the Roman legions under Titus razed the temple in AD 70, with Josephus reporting that the soldiers, seeking gold that had melted between the stones during the fire, literally pried apart every stone (Jewish War 7.1-4). The phrase ΤΑΥΤΑ Α ΘΕΩΡΕΙΤΕ ("these things which you behold") uses ΘΕΩΡΕΩ ("to observe, to contemplate") — the very act of admiring the temple becomes the occasion for the prophecy of its destruction.

6. **21:8 — "See that you be not led astray" (blepete mē planēthēte):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΛΕΠΕΤΕ ΜΗ ΠΛΑΝΗΘΗΤΕ. The verb ΠΛΑΝΑΩ ("to lead astray, to cause to wander, to deceive") — from ΠΛΑΝΗ ("wandering, error") — carries the imagery of a traveler led off the path. The passive voice ΠΛΑΝΗΘΗΤΕ ("be led astray") implies external agents of deception. Jesus identifies these agents: ΠΟΛΛΟΙ ΓΑΡ ΕΛΕΥΣΟΝΤΑΙ ΕΠΙ ΤΩ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΙ ΜΟΥ ("for many shall come in my name"), claiming both messianic identity (ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ, "I am he") and prophetic authority (Ο ΚΑΙΡΟΣ ΗΓΓΙΚΕΝ, "the time has drawn near"). The command ΜΗ ΠΟΡΕΥΘΗΤΕ ΟΠΙΣΩ ΑΥΤΩΝ ("do not go after them") uses the aorist subjunctive with ΜΗ to prohibit even a single act of following. The discourse that follows (vv. 8-36) constitutes the Lukan Olivet Discourse, parallel to Mark 13 and Matthew 24-25 but with significant Lukan distinctives, particularly the explicit reference to Jerusalem's siege (vv. 20-24).

7. **21:9 — "wars and tumults" (polemous kai akatastasias):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΟΛΕΜΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΑΚΑΤΑΣΤΑΣΙΑΣ. Where Mark 13:7 and Matthew 24:6 have ΠΟΛΕΜΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΑΚΟΑΣ ΠΟΛΕΜΩΝ ("wars and rumors of wars"), Luke substitutes ΑΚΑΤΑΣΤΑΣΙΑΣ ("tumults, disorders, instabilities") — from Α (negative) + ΚΑΘΙΣΤΗΜΙ ("to set in order, to establish") — a word denoting civic upheaval and political instability. This Lukan substitution may reflect the period between Jesus's prophecy and the fall of Jerusalem, which was marked by assassinations, revolts, and factional violence. The phrase ΟΥΚ ΕΥΘΕΩΣ ΤΟ ΤΕΛΟΣ ("the end is not immediately") is Luke's characteristic addition: he distinguishes between the penultimate signs and the ultimate consummation, resisting the conflation of Jerusalem's fall with the end of the age — a distinction crucial for Luke's two-volume narrative, which extends salvation history through Acts and beyond AD 70.

8. **21:11 — "terrors and great signs from heaven" (phobētra te kai sēmeia ap' ouranou megala):** Sinaiticus reads ΦΟΒΗΤΡΑ ΤΕ ΚΑΙ ΣΗΜΕΙΑ ΑΠ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΥ ΜΕΓΑΛΑ. The noun ΦΟΒΗΤΡΟΝ ("terror, fearful sight") — from ΦΟΒΕΩ ("to frighten") — appears only here in the New Testament. This is a uniquely Lukan addition to the eschatological discourse: neither Mark nor Matthew includes "terrors" alongside the cosmic signs. Luke also adds ΛΟΙΜΟΙ ("pestilences") to the list of calamities — ΣΕΙΣΜΟΙ ΤΕ ΜΕΓΑΛΟΙ ΚΑΙ ΚΑΤΑ ΤΟΠΟΥΣ ΛΙΜΟΙ ΚΑΙ ΛΟΙΜΟΙ ("great earthquakes and in various places famines and pestilences"). The near-homophony of ΛΙΜΟΙ ("famines") and ΛΟΙΜΟΙ ("pestilences") may reflect a traditional pairing in Greek apocalyptic language. The phrase ΚΑΤΑ ΤΟΠΟΥΣ ("in various places," literally "according to places") distributes the calamities geographically: they will not be concentrated in one location but scattered throughout the inhabited world.

9. **21:12 — "delivering you up to the synagogues and prisons" (paradidontes eis tas synagōgas kai phylakas):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΑΔΙΔΟΝΤΕΣ ΕΙΣ ΤΑΣ ΣΥΝΑΓΩΓΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΦΥΛΑΚΑΣ. The verb ΠΑΡΑΔΙΔΩΜΙ ("to deliver up, to hand over, to betray") — from ΠΑΡΑ ("alongside, over") + ΔΙΔΩΜΙ ("to give") — is the same word used for Judas's betrayal of Jesus (22:4, 6) and for Jesus being "delivered up" to the authorities (24:7). The disciples will share their master's experience of betrayal and arraignment. The dual venues — ΣΥΝΑΓΩΓΑΣ ("synagogues," Jewish judicial proceedings) and ΦΥΛΑΚΑΣ ("prisons," Roman custody) — anticipate the narrative of Acts, where the apostles face both Jewish courts (Acts 4:1-22; 5:17-42; 6:12-7:60) and Roman authorities (Acts 16:19-40; 24-26). The phrase ΠΡΟ ΔΕ ΤΟΥΤΩΝ ΠΑΝΤΩΝ ("but before all these things") is significant: the persecution of the disciples precedes the cosmic calamities, establishing a chronological priority — the church's suffering comes first, then the fall of Jerusalem, then the cosmic signs.

10. **21:14-15 — "not to practice beforehand to make a defense" (mē promeletan apologēthēnai):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΗ ΠΡΟΜΕΛΕΤΑΝ ΑΠΟΛΟΓΗΘΗΝΑΙ. The verb ΠΡΟΜΕΛΕΤΑΩ ("to practice beforehand, to rehearse in advance") — from ΠΡΟ ("before") + ΜΕΛΕΤΑΩ ("to practice, to meditate upon, to rehearse") — appears only here in the New Testament. The noun implied by ΑΠΟΛΟΓΗΘΗΝΑΙ is ΑΠΟΛΟΓΙΑ ("defense, defense speech"), the formal legal defense presented before a tribunal. The promise ΕΓΩ ΓΑΡ ΔΩΣΩ ΥΜΙΝ ΣΤΟΜΑ ΚΑΙ ΣΟΦΙΑΝ ("for I will give you a mouth and wisdom") is distinctively Lukan: where Mark 13:11 attributes the words to the Holy Spirit, Luke has Jesus himself as the source. The word ΣΤΟΜΑ ("mouth") is a metonym for speech — not merely the faculty but the content and the eloquence. The compound promise — ΣΤΟΜΑ ΚΑΙ ΣΟΦΙΑΝ ("mouth and wisdom") — combines the capacity to speak with the substance of what is spoken. The result: Η ΟΥ ΔΥΝΗΣΟΝΤΑΙ ΑΝΤΙΣΤΗΝΑΙ Η ΑΝΤΕΙΠΕΙΝ ("which they shall not be able to withstand or to contradict") — ΑΝΤΙΣΤΗΝΑΙ ("to withstand," from ΑΝΤΙ + ΙΣΤΗΜΙ) addresses force; ΑΝΤΕΙΠΕΙΝ ("to contradict," from ΑΝΤΙ + ΛΕΓΩ) addresses argument. Neither power nor rhetoric will overcome the disciples' testimony.

11. **21:16-17 — "delivered up even by parents and brothers" (paradothēsesthe kai hypo goneōn kai adelphōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΑΔΟΘΗΣΕΣΘΕ ΚΑΙ ΥΠΟ ΓΟΝΕΩΝ ΚΑΙ ΑΔΕΛΦΩΝ. The catalogue of betrayers — ΓΟΝΕΩΝ ("parents"), ΑΔΕΛΦΩΝ ("brothers"), ΣΥΓΓΕΝΩΝ ("kinsmen"), ΦΙΛΩΝ ("friends") — exhausts the categories of human relationship: family by blood (parents, brothers, kinsmen) and family by choice (friends). The conjunction ΚΑΙ ("even") before ΥΠΟ ΓΟΝΕΩΝ ("by parents") intensifies the shock: even the most sacred human bonds will be broken. The phrase ΘΑΝΑΤΩΣΟΥΣΙΝ ΕΞ ΥΜΩΝ ("they shall put to death some from among you") — ΘΑΝΑΤΟΩ ("to put to death") with the partitive ΕΞ ΥΜΩΝ ("from among you") — indicates that martyrdom will not be universal but selective. The paradox of verses 16-18 is stark: some will be killed (v. 16), yet not a hair of your head shall perish (v. 18). The resolution lies in the distinction between physical death and ultimate destruction: ΑΠΟΛΛΥΜΙ in verse 18 ("perish, be destroyed") refers to final, eschatological loss, not temporal death. Physical martyrdom does not constitute ΑΠΩΛΕΙΑ ("perdition") for the faithful.

12. **21:19 — "In your endurance you shall gain your souls" (en tē hypomonē hymōn ktēsasthe tas psychas hymōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΗ ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗ ΥΜΩΝ ΚΤΗΣΑΣΘΕ ΤΑΣ ΨΥΧΑΣ ΥΜΩΝ. The noun ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗ ("endurance, patient steadfastness") — from ΥΠΟ ("under") + ΜΕΝΩ ("to remain, to abide") — denotes the capacity to remain standing under pressure, not passive resignation but active perseverance. The verb ΚΤΑΟΜΑΙ ("to gain, to acquire, to possess") in the imperative ΚΤΗΣΑΣΘΕ means "gain" or "acquire" — the soul is not merely preserved but won through endurance. Some manuscripts read the future indicative ΚΤΗΣΕΣΘΕ ("you shall gain"), transforming the command into a promise; Sinaiticus appears to support the imperative form, which makes endurance a moral demand rather than an automatic outcome. The noun ΨΥΧΗ ("soul, life, self") encompasses both the present life and the eternal self — by enduring faithfully, the disciple secures not merely survival but the integrity of the whole person before God.

13. **21:20 — "Jerusalem being surrounded by armies" (kykloumenēn hypo stratopedōn Ierousalēm):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΥΚΛΟΥΜΕΝΗΝ ΥΠΟ ΣΤΡΑΤΟΠΕΔΩΝ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ. This is Luke's most distinctive contribution to the eschatological discourse: where Mark 13:14 and Matthew 24:15 have the enigmatic "abomination of desolation standing where it ought not," Luke substitutes a concrete military reference. The verb ΚΥΚΛΟΩ ("to encircle, to surround") — from ΚΥΚΛΟΣ ("circle") — describes a military siege. The noun ΣΤΡΑΤΟΠΕΔΟΝ ("army, encampment") — from ΣΤΡΑΤΟΣ ("army") + ΠΕΔΟΝ ("ground, plain") — denotes an army in the field, deployed for battle. The phrase ΗΓΓΙΚΕΝ Η ΕΡΗΜΩΣΙΣ ΑΥΤΗΣ ("her desolation has drawn near") uses ΕΡΗΜΩΣΙΣ ("desolation, devastation") — from ΕΡΗΜΟΩ ("to make desolate") — the same root as in Daniel 9:27 LXX (ΒΔΕΛΥΓΜΑ ΤΩΝ ΕΡΗΜΩΣΕΩΝ, "abomination of desolations"). Luke interprets the Danielic prophecy through the lens of the Roman siege, making explicit what Mark left cryptic. The pronoun ΑΥΤΗΣ ("her") personalizes Jerusalem: the city is addressed as a person whose desolation is approaching.

14. **21:22 — "days of vengeance" (hēmerai ekdikēseōs):** Sinaiticus reads ΗΜΕΡΑΙ ΕΚΔΙΚΗΣΕΩΣ. The noun ΕΚΔΙΚΗΣΙΣ ("vengeance, retribution, vindication") — from ΕΚ ("out of") + ΔΙΚΗ ("justice, right") — denotes the execution of justice, the righteous recompense for persistent violation of the covenant. The phrase echoes Hosea 9:7 LXX: ΗΚΑΣΙΝ ΑΙ ΗΜΕΡΑΙ ΤΗΣ ΕΚΔΙΚΗΣΕΩΣ ("the days of vengeance have come"). The purpose clause ΤΟΥ ΠΛΗΣΘΗΝΑΙ ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΑ ΓΕΓΡΑΜΜΕΝΑ ("that all things that are written may be fulfilled") — from ΠΙΜΠΛΗΜΙ ("to fill, to fulfill") — presents Jerusalem's destruction not as a random catastrophe but as the fulfillment of prophetic scripture. The passive ΤΑ ΓΕΓΡΑΜΜΕΝΑ ("the things that have been written") is a divine passive: what God has caused to be written in the prophets will now be accomplished. The scope is comprehensive: ΠΑΝΤΑ ("all things") — not merely one prophecy but the entire prophetic tradition concerning Jerusalem's judgment (Deuteronomy 28:49-57; Isaiah 63:4; Jeremiah 5:29; Daniel 9:26-27).

15. **21:24 — "Jerusalem shall be trampled by the nations" (Ierousalēm estai patoumenē hypo ethnōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ ΕΣΤΑΙ ΠΑΤΟΥΜΕΝΗ ΥΠΟ ΕΘΝΩΝ. The periphrastic future ΕΣΤΑΙ ΠΑΤΟΥΜΕΝΗ ("shall be being trampled") — the present participle with the future of ΕΙΜΙ — emphasizes the ongoing, continuous nature of the trampling: it is not a single event but an enduring condition. The verb ΠΑΤΕΩ ("to tread, to trample") echoes the language of Daniel 8:13 LXX and Zechariah 12:3 LXX. The phrase ΑΧΡΙ ΟΥ ΠΛΗΡΩΘΩΣΙΝ ΚΑΙΡΟΙ ΕΘΝΩΝ ("until the times of the nations be fulfilled") introduces one of the most theologically significant concepts in Luke's eschatology: the ΚΑΙΡΟΙ ΕΘΝΩΝ ("times of the nations/Gentiles"). This phrase implies a divinely appointed period during which Gentile dominion over Jerusalem will continue — and by implication, that this period has a terminus. The noun ΚΑΙΡΟΣ ("appointed time, season") — as distinct from ΧΡΟΝΟΣ ("duration, time in general") — suggests not mere chronological passage but divinely measured epochs. The passive ΠΛΗΡΩΘΩΣΙΝ ("be fulfilled") is again a divine passive: God fills the measure of the Gentile age. This verse is unique to Luke and has no parallel in Mark or Matthew.

16. **21:25-26 — "distress of nations in perplexity" (synochē ethnōn en aporia):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΝΟΧΗ ΕΘΝΩΝ ΕΝ ΑΠΟΡΙΑ. The noun ΣΥΝΟΧΗ ("distress, anguish, constraint") — from ΣΥΝΕΧΩ ("to hold together, to press together, to constrain") — describes the condition of being hemmed in with no escape. The noun ΑΠΟΡΙΑ ("perplexity, bewilderment") — from Α (negative) + ΠΟΡΟΣ ("passage, way through") — literally means "no way through" — a state of total intellectual and practical helplessness. Together they describe nations gripped by inescapable anguish and unable to find any solution. The cause is cosmic: ΗΧΟΥΣ ΘΑΛΑΣΣΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΣΑΛΟΥ ("the roaring of the sea and the surging") — ΗΧΟΣ ("sound, roaring") and ΣΑΛΟΣ ("tossing, agitation, surging") depict the ocean in chaotic upheaval, an image drawn from the ancient Near Eastern association of the sea with primordial chaos (cf. Psalm 46:3; Isaiah 17:12-13). Verse 26 intensifies: ΑΠΟΨΥΧΟΝΤΩΝ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ ΑΠΟ ΦΟΒΟΥ ("men fainting from fear") — the verb ΑΠΟΨΥΧΩ ("to breathe out, to faint, to expire") — from ΑΠΟ ("away from") + ΨΥΧΗ ("breath, life") — appears only here in the New Testament and describes the loss of consciousness or even life itself from sheer terror.

17. **21:27 — "the Son of Man coming in a cloud" (ton Huion tou Anthrōpou erchomenon en nephelē):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΥΙΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΝ ΝΕΦΕΛΗ. The singular ΝΕΦΕΛΗ ("cloud") — where Mark 13:26 has ΕΝ ΝΕΦΕΛΑΙΣ ("in clouds," plural) — may reflect Daniel 7:13 LXX more precisely: ΜΕΤΑ ΤΩΝ ΝΕΦΕΛΩΝ ΤΟΥ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΥ ΩΣ ΥΙΟΣ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΣ ΗΝ ("with the clouds of heaven as a son of man he was coming"). The title Ο ΥΙΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥ ("the Son of Man") is Jesus's self-designation throughout the Gospels; here it carries its full apocalyptic weight from Daniel 7, where the "one like a son of man" receives from the Ancient of Days dominion, glory, and an everlasting kingdom. The triad ΔΥΝΑΜΙΣ ΚΑΙ ΔΟΞΑ ΠΟΛΛΗ ("power and great glory") — ΔΥΝΑΜΙΣ ("power, might") is the raw capacity; ΔΟΞΑ ("glory, splendor") is the visible manifestation of divine majesty. The coming is not hidden or ambiguous but public and unmistakable — a reversal of the humble first advent.

18. **21:28 — "straighten yourselves and lift up your heads" (anakupsate kai eparate tas kephalas hymōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΑΚΥΨΑΤΕ ΚΑΙ ΕΠΑΡΑΤΕ ΤΑΣ ΚΕΦΑΛΑΣ ΥΜΩΝ. The verb ΑΝΑΚΥΠΤΩ ("to straighten up, to raise oneself") — from ΑΝΑ ("up") + ΚΥΠΤΩ ("to bend, to stoop") — commands the posture of the liberated: where the oppressed and fearful bend low, the redeemed stand tall. The noun ΑΠΟΛΥΤΡΩΣΙΣ ("redemption, release, deliverance") — from ΑΠΟΛΥΤΡΟΩ ("to release on payment of ransom") — carries its full Old Testament resonance: God's redemption of Israel from Egypt (Exodus 6:6; Deuteronomy 7:8), the redemption of property and persons in the Jubilee year (Leviticus 25), and the prophetic promise of final deliverance (Isaiah 35:10; 51:11). The paradox is deliberate: the very events that cause the nations to faint with terror (v. 26) are the occasion for the disciples to stand with confidence, because those events signal not judgment upon them but redemption for them. The verb ΕΓΓΙΖΕΙ ("draws near") — present tense — makes the redemption vivid and imminent, not a distant future hope but an approaching reality.

19. **21:29 — "the fig tree and all the trees" (tēn sukēn kai panta ta dendra):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΗΝ ΣΥΚΗΝ ΚΑΙ ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΑ ΔΕΝΔΡΑ. Luke's addition of ΚΑΙ ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΑ ΔΕΝΔΡΑ ("and all the trees") is absent from Mark 13:28 and Matthew 24:32, which mention only the fig tree. This Lukan expansion generalizes the parable: any deciduous tree serves the illustration, not the fig tree exclusively. This may also serve to prevent an exclusively allegorical reading where the fig tree represents Israel alone. The verb ΠΡΟΒΑΛΛΩ ("to put forth, to send out") — from ΠΡΟ ("forth") + ΒΑΛΛΩ ("to throw, to cast") — describes the emergence of leaves from buds. The observation is from nature: ΒΛΕΠΟΝΤΕΣ ΑΦ ΕΑΥΤΩΝ ΓΙΝΩΣΚΕΤΕ ("seeing, you know of yourselves") — the knowledge is self-evident, requiring no expert interpretation. Just as budding trees are a reliable sign of approaching summer, the events described in vv. 8-27 are reliable signs of the approaching kingdom.

20. **21:32 — "this generation shall not pass away" (ou mē parelthē hē genea hautē):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥ ΜΗ ΠΑΡΕΛΘΗ Η ΓΕΝΕΑ ΑΥΤΗ. The double negative ΟΥ ΜΗ with the aorist subjunctive ΠΑΡΕΛΘΗ constitutes the most emphatic form of negation in Greek: "shall by no means pass away." The noun ΓΕΝΕΑ ("generation") — from ΓΕΝΟΣ ("race, kind, offspring") — is among the most debated terms in New Testament eschatology. It may denote: (a) the contemporaries of Jesus, meaning these events will occur within their lifetime — fulfilled in the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70; (b) the Jewish people as a whole, meaning Israel will endure until all is accomplished; or (c) the generation that witnesses the beginning of these signs, meaning those who see the signs will also see the consummation. The formal translation preserves the ambiguity of the Greek without resolving it. The verb ΠΑΡΕΡΧΟΜΑΙ ("to pass away, to pass by") is then applied to heaven and earth in verse 33 — the same verb used for the passing of a generation is used for the passing of the cosmos, creating a rhetorical contrast: the created order will pass, but Christ's words will not.

21. **21:34 — "weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness" (barēthōsin hai kardiai hymōn en kraipalē kai methē):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΑΡΗΘΩΣΙΝ ΑΙ ΚΑΡΔΙΑΙ ΥΜΩΝ ΕΝ ΚΡΑΙΠΑΛΗ ΚΑΙ ΜΕΘΗ. The verb ΒΑΡΕΩ ("to weigh down, to burden, to make heavy") — from ΒΑΡΟΣ ("weight") — describes the dulling of spiritual perception under the burden of excess. The noun ΚΡΑΙΠΑΛΗ ("dissipation, hangover, carousing") appears only here in the New Testament; it denotes the stupor and nausea following excessive drinking — not the act of drunkenness but its debilitating aftermath. Paired with ΜΕΘΗ ("drunkenness, intoxication"), the two words cover the full cycle of excess: the act and its consequences. The third element — ΜΕΡΙΜΝΑΙΣ ΒΙΩΤΙΚΑΙΣ ("cares of this life") — broadens the warning beyond literal drunkenness: the adjective ΒΙΩΤΙΚΟΣ ("pertaining to daily life," from ΒΙΟΣ, "livelihood") identifies everyday anxieties as equally deadening to spiritual alertness. The image of the ΠΑΓΙΣ ("snare, trap") in the following clause — ΑΙΦΝΙΔΙΟΣ ΕΦ ΥΜΑΣ ΕΠΙΣΤΗ ΕΚΕΙΝΗ Η ΗΜΕΡΑ ΩΣ ΠΑΓΙΣ ("that day come upon you suddenly as a snare") — draws from hunting imagery: the unsuspecting animal is caught because it was not watchful.

22. **21:36 — "be watchful at every season, praying" (agrypneite en panti kairō deomenoi):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΓΡΥΠΝΕΙΤΕ ΕΝ ΠΑΝΤΙ ΚΑΙΡΩ ΔΕΟΜΕΝΟΙ. The verb ΑΓΡΥΠΝΕΩ ("to be sleepless, to keep watch, to be vigilant") — from Α (negative) + ΥΠΝΟΣ ("sleep") — literally means "to be without sleep," metaphorically "to be constantly alert." This is stronger than the more common ΓΡΗΓΟΡΕΩ ("to stay awake, to watch"); ΑΓΡΥΠΝΕΩ implies unceasing, effortful vigilance. The participle ΔΕΟΜΕΝΟΙ ("praying, beseeching") — from ΔΕΟΜΑΙ ("to ask, to beseech, to pray") — defines the mode of watchfulness: it is not anxious scanning of the horizon but prayerful dependence on God. The purpose is twofold: ΚΑΤΙΣΧΥΣΑΙ ΕΚΦΥΓΕΙΝ ("to have strength to escape") and ΣΤΑΘΗΝΑΙ ΕΜΠΡΟΣΘΕΝ ΤΟΥ ΥΙΟΥ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥ ("to stand before the Son of Man"). The verb ΚΑΤΙΣΧΥΩ ("to have strength, to prevail") — from ΚΑΤΑ (intensifier) + ΙΣΧΥΩ ("to be strong") — implies that escaping the coming tribulation requires divine strength, not human effort. The final image — standing (ΣΤΑΘΗΝΑΙ, aorist passive of ΙΣΤΗΜΙ) before the Son of Man — echoes the command of verse 28 to "straighten yourselves": the chapter's exhortation culminates in the posture of the redeemed before their judge and deliverer.

23. **21:37-38 — "he was teaching by day ... lodged on the mount called Olivet" (ēn didaskon ... ēulizeto eis to oros to kaloumenon Elaiōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΗΝ ΔΙΔΑΣΚΩΝ ... ΗΥΛΙΖΕΤΟ ΕΙΣ ΤΟ ΟΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΛΑΙΩΝ. The imperfect periphrastic ΗΝ ΔΙΔΑΣΚΩΝ ("he was teaching") describes habitual, ongoing activity — this was Jesus's daily pattern during his final week. The verb ΑΥΛΙΖΟΜΑΙ ("to lodge, to spend the night, to bivouac") — from ΑΥΛΗ ("courtyard, open-air enclosure") — suggests sleeping in the open or in simple accommodation, not in a house. The Mount of Olives (ΤΟ ΟΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΛΑΙΩΝ, "the mount called of Olives") served as Jesus's nocturnal retreat — the same location where the agony in the garden will occur (22:39). The verse ΠΑΣΔΕ Ο ΛΑΟΣ ΩΡΘΡΙΖΕΝ ΠΡΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΝ ("all the people came early in the morning to him") uses ΟΡΘΡΙΖΩ ("to rise early, to come at dawn") — from ΟΡΘΡΟΣ ("dawn, early morning") — a verb appearing only here in the New Testament. The people's eagerness to hear Jesus contrasts sharply with the leaders' eagerness to destroy him, maintaining the tension established since 19:47-48 between popular devotion and official hostility. These two summary verses close the public teaching ministry: after this, the narrative moves to the Passover plot (22:1-6) and the Upper Room.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **The widow's offering as thematic hinge (vv. 1-4):** The formal translation preserves the direct connection between 20:47 (scribes devouring widows' houses) and 21:1-4 (a widow giving her last coins). No chapter break exists in the original manuscript; the chapter division is a medieval convenience that obscures the irony Luke constructs. The formal rendering maintains the vocabulary links: ΒΙΟΣ ("livelihood") in 21:4 corresponds to the ΟΙΚΙΑΙ ("houses") of widows in 20:47 — the religious system consumes what little the poor possess, yet this particular widow gives voluntarily what the scribes would take by exploitation. The translation resists the temptation to sentimentalize the widow; the Greek presents her action with austere simplicity.

2. **The eschatological discourse as structured prophecy (vv. 5-36):** The formal translation preserves Luke's careful temporal markers — ΠΡΟ ΔΕ ΤΟΥΤΩΝ ΠΑΝΤΩΝ ("but before all these things," v. 12), ΤΟΤΕ ("then," vv. 10, 20, 21, 27), ΑΧΡΙ ΟΥ ("until," v. 24) — which create a chronological framework absent from Mark's more fluid account. Luke distinguishes three phases: (a) persecution of the church (vv. 12-19), (b) the fall of Jerusalem (vv. 20-24), and (c) cosmic signs and the Son of Man's coming (vv. 25-28). The formal translation maintains these structural markers without interpretive smoothing, allowing the reader to perceive the phased eschatology that is one of Luke's most distinctive theological contributions.

3. **Jerusalem personified (vv. 20-24):** The formal translation preserves the shift from impersonal prophecy to personal address when Jerusalem enters the discourse. The pronoun ΑΥΤΗΣ ("her") in verse 20, the commands to those "in the midst of her" (v. 21), and the language of trampling (v. 24) all treat Jerusalem as a living entity suffering violation. This personification echoes the lament of 13:34 ("Jerusalem, Jerusalem") and anticipates 19:41-44 (Jesus weeping over the city). The formal rendering retains this personification rather than converting it to abstract language about "the city."

4. **Endurance as eschatological virtue (v. 19):** The translation of ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗ as "endurance" rather than "patience" preserves the active, strenuous character of the Greek word. ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗ is not passive waiting but active remaining — standing firm under assault. This is the virtue that connects the promise of divine protection (v. 18, "not a hair of your head shall perish") with the reality of martyrdom (v. 16, "they shall put to death some from among you"): the endurance required is not the avoidance of suffering but faithfulness through and despite suffering, with the assurance that physical death does not constitute ultimate loss.

5. **Continuity with Luke 20:** The temple setting continues unbroken from 20:1 through 21:38. Jesus's teaching ministry in the temple (19:47; 20:1; 21:37) frames the entire section as a sustained confrontation between Jesus and the religious establishment, with the people caught between. The widow's offering (21:1-4) concludes the theme of true versus false piety begun with the parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector (18:9-14). The prophecy of the temple's destruction (21:5-6) brings to completion the judgment announced in the vineyard parable (20:9-18) and the weeping over Jerusalem (19:41-44). The formal translation maintains these thematic continuities through consistent vocabulary: ΔΙΔΑΣΚΩ ("to teach"), ΙΕΡΟΝ ("temple"), ΛΑΟΣ ("people"), and ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΙΣ/ΑΡΧΙΕΡΕΙΣ ("scribes/chief priests") appear with the same renderings throughout chapters 19-21.
